The safest teacher solution is a compact, reproducible text-classification workflow
that separates tokenization, probability estimation, and evaluation cleanly.

## Suggested approach

1. Load the SMS Spam Collection with `pandas.read_csv(..., sep="\t")` and map the
   target to a clear binary coding, for example `ham=0`, `spam=1`.
2. Use one deterministic, stratified train/test split so the spam base rate stays
   comparable across runs.
3. Fit `CountVectorizer()` on the training messages only and transform the test set
   with the fitted vocabulary.
4. Estimate the class priors from the training labels and estimate class-conditional
   token probabilities with additive smoothing so zero counts do not collapse the log
   scores.
5. Score each test message with log-posteriors of the form
   `log P(C_k) + sum_j x_j log P(token_j | C_k)` and predict the class with the
   larger score.
6. Evaluate the manual classifier with a confusion matrix plus accuracy, precision,
   and recall.
7. Fit `MultinomialNB()` on the same count matrix and compare the results directly to
   the manual implementation.

## What students should usually find

- The spam class is the minority class, so raw accuracy alone can hide bad filtering
  behavior.
- A simple multinomial naive Bayes model often performs surprisingly well on this
  dataset because token counts carry strong signal for promotional language.
- Words tied to prizes, free offers, urgent calls to action, and reply instructions
  usually receive strong spam weight, while conversational words are more common in
  ham.
- The manual implementation and `MultinomialNB` should land in the same performance
  neighborhood when smoothing and tokenization are handled consistently.

## Teaching emphasis

The important point is not whether students optimize the best possible score.
The important point is that they can explain the full pipeline: split, vectorize,
estimate, predict, and evaluate.

Use the debrief to stress two things:

- precision and recall matter more than a single overall accuracy number in a spam
  setting,
- the conditional-independence assumption is unrealistic for language, but still
  useful when the goal is a fast, interpretable baseline.
